title: Making the Rules
learningGoal: The learner will compose sentences demonstrating strict adherence to the bare infinitive rule following modal auxiliaries, successfully generating semantic distinctions between permissive, advisory, and obligatory statements.
instructions: You are the manager of a Library! Write 3 rules for the people reading books. Sentence 1: Tell them something they are ALLOWED to do using 'You can'. Sentence 2: Give them ADVICE using 'You should'. Sentence 3: Tell them a STRICT RULE of what is forbidden, using 'You must not'.

contentItems.text: CRITICAL RULE: Never use 'To', '-ing', or '-ed' after Can, Should, or Must!

writingPrompt: Write 3 sentences for a Library. 1. You can... 2. You should... 3. You must not...
promptGoal: Test independent syntactic production of bare infinitives following modal auxiliaries while assessing semantic accuracy of 'can', 'should', and 'must'.
referenceHints: Sentence 3 (Strict Rule): You must not [action verb] loudly!

modelExampleOutputs.targetText: You can read books here. You should speak quietly. You must not yell.
modelExampleOutputs.transliteration: यू कैन रीड बुक्स हियर। यू शुड स्पीक क्वाइटली। यू मस्ट नॉट येल।
modelExampleOutputs.baseTranslation: आप यहाँ किताबें पढ़ सकते हैं। आपको चुपचाप बात करनी चाहिए। आपको चिल्लाना नहीं चाहिए।
modelExampleOutputs.annotation: Perfect score. The learner maintained the bare infinitive across all three sentences (can read, should speak, must not yell) and accurately mapped the semantic hierarchy of the modals.

modelExampleOutputs.targetText: You can to read books. You should speaking quietly. You must to not yell.
modelExampleOutputs.transliteration: यू कैन टू रीड बुक्स। यू शुड स्पीकिंग क्वाइटली। यू मस्ट टू नॉट येल।
modelExampleOutputs.annotation: Critical failure. Massive L1 interference leading to 'to' insertions ('can to read', 'must to not') and verb inflection ('should speaking').

evaluationCriteria.description: Did the user aggressively maintain clean verbs? Heavy penalization for any instance of 'to', '-ing', '-ed', or '-s' following 'can', 'should', or 'must'.
Does the 'can' sentence show permission, the 'should' sentence show advice, and the 'must not' sentence show a strict rule?
Were all 3 sentences generated using the 3 requested modals?

adminCorrectAnswerSet.sampleAnswer: You can take computers. You should read fast. You must not eat food.
acceptableAlternates: Can sit here. Should be quiet. Must not sleep.
adminCorrectAnswerSet.grammarBaseline: Absolute zero tolerance for 'to' verbs. 'I can to swim' instantly fails.

feedbackRules.onPass: Magnificent! You wrote perfect rules! Most importantly, you proved you have mastered the Golden Rule: the action verb NEVER, EVER changes form after a modal!
feedbackRules.onFail: Please review your writing. If you wrote 'You can to read' or 'You must not eating', you failed the core rule. No prefixes, no suffixes. Just Can + Action! Ask for a hint to see the exact structure.
feedbackRules.onPartial: Good start! Double check the difference between Should and Must. Must is for things that will get you kicked out of the library! Should is just friendly advice.

audioAssets.audioTranscript: Write library rules. One sentence with Can. One with Should. One with Must Not.

hintText: Keep it simple! You can read. You should whisper. You must not eat.
